dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 1 responding for a reported Burns/Explosion call at Redacted Address in Richmond.
Hello, I'm Jordan, an EMT with the rescue team. What happened to make you call 911 today?
My son burned himself with hot noodles. I called because I was really worried.
Is he awake and aware right now?
Yes, he's awake and crying.
Does he respond to sounds or your voice?
He reaches out for things, even now.
His airway looks clear and not blocked.
He's breathing pretty fast. Let me count it.
He's breathing real fast-around 43 per minute-that's tachypnea.
Respirations are at 43 once and now at 45-rapid breathing, non-labored.
His skin looks good-normal color, warm, and dry.
Heart rate is 160, which is fast. Pulse is strong and easily palpable.
Blood pressure is 140 over 76. That's still within range for a child.
His oxygen saturation is 100% on room air.
The EKG shows sinus tachycardia with a rate of around 160.
Could you please provide your child's name, his birthdate, and his current weight?
His name is Sam, he's one year old and about 14 kilograms.
Can you tell us what exactly happened?
He pulled a bowl that had some boiling water. It mainly had noodles with a little water.
When was the last time he had food or drink?
I think it was earlier, but I don't remember exactly.
If 0 means no pain and 10 is the worst, how would you rate his pain now?
I'd say it's a 10-he just won't stop crying.
Where's the burn located, and how large is the area affected?
It's mainly on his left shoulder and armpit, spreading a little to his left chest.
I'll take a closer look to assess the severity and coverage.
Confirming his GCS: 15 total-eye 4, verbal 5, motor 6. That means he's acting age-appropriate.
I'm going to cover the burn with a sterile dressing now. Would you like to watch?
I'll wrap him in a sterile burn sheet to prevent heat loss and protect the wound.
I'm following protocol 10-2 for thermal burns. I'll place him on a cardiac monitor to check for arrhythmias.
Installing 4-lead ECG per protocol now.
Since he's in significant pain, I'm going to give him fentanyl-1 mcg/kg intranasal.
Splitting the 13 mcg dose in each nostril-it's within standing orders.
Evaluated burn coverage-3-4% second degree with some sloughing in the left axilla and 9-10% first degree elsewhere.
I tried to obtain a glucose, but we decided to go straight to pain management based on his presentation.
Respirations are still quick-45 per minute, but no labored breathing.
His blood pressure is now 113/68.
Pulse has dropped to 132-still rapid but less than before.
SpO2 is 99%-good oxygen levels.
He seems to be settling down more. Have you noticed any improvement in his crying?
Yes, he's a lot more calm, so that's good.
We're transporting him to VCU Health Systems. They have the best burn care for children.
I'd like to go with him again. He feels safer with me.
Of course-would you be comfortable in the harness next to the car seat?
I'm securing the car seat onto the stretcher-everything is locked in place.
We've wrapped him in a burn sheet and replaced the diaper after a full skin check.
You're doing a great job-thank you so much.
The hospital has been notified and is preparing the pediatrics department.
The burn assessment, vitals, and EKG are all on the report. I'll provide a full summary en route.
Current pulse is 128, still elevated, but beginning to stabilize.
Respirations are slightly lower at 45 and still steady.
Blood pressure is 109/58 and SpO2 is 98%. EKG still shows sinus tachycardia.
I know he's in good hands. You've all been amazing.
We'll hand him over with all the documentation and full burn sheet coverage.
No allergy reactions noted, and all pain management and monitoring followed protocol.
Sam is 14 kg, secured in his car seat with the EKG running and burn sheet in place.
Fentanyl is helping him-pain is under control now.
I can't thank you enough. You were quick, steady, and kind.
Thanks, Ma'am. That's what we do here-keep him safe on the way in.
Vital signs are stable for transport, and we're en route with full pediatric resources.
Monitoring him continuously-we're all staying sharp on this call.
Tachypnea is down from 43 to 45-still present but slowed a bit.
His motor function remains intact. He's responding well to touch and voice.
I'm taking a final set of vitals before sign-off at the hospital.
Heart rate is 128, breathe is 45, blood pressure 109/58. SpO2 is 98%.
EKG still shows sinus tach, no arrhythmias reported.
All signs are trending in the right direction.
He's fully bundled-warm, comfortable, and secure in the car seat.
I'm multi-casting the hospital with updated vitals and burn assessment.
All support systems are in place. We'll do a full hand-off in the ED.
This is the best hospital I know. Thank you for trusting us.
Thank you, Ma'am. Our team and the ED are ready.
Healthy baby to a healthy facility. This one's a good one.
Keep your eyes on vitals while I secure the burn sheet and case report.
All documentation and medical steps are in order-kids are being handled safely.
I'm right next to him. He's safe with you and me both.
We're ready to wheels-up. Door's locked, lights and sirens green.
Let's move and prepare for the hospital hand-over in 15 minutes.
Go easy on the roads-he's still a baby.
Copy that. Slow and smooth during the ride.
I'm giving updates to the ED on every vital every five minutes.
Arrival estimate is seven minutes at normal traffic.
Fentanyl is working. He's quiet and conjugating in his blanket wrap.
Remaining on his seat-never moving unless absolutely necessary.
I think he's beginning to rest more now that the pain is under control.
Yes-it helped so much so quickly.
Good to hear. Makes our job easier-and it's what we do.
Anyone need a stretch before we arrive?
No. Excellent condition and response in the ER is the focus.
I'll be the first to thank the ED for whatever care he gets next.
I'm sure they'll be ready. He's got a full travel team.
Keep a hand near his upper body just in case we need to move quickly.
Unit time is monitored via GPS. Keep radio silent unless emergency.
Radio check is clean. No incoming from PD or incident repeat.
Car seat stability checked every 2 minutes. No signs of movement noted.
Hospital ETA down to 5 minutes. Should arrive during their full team time.
I'm updating the care report as we go-no missing parameters.
Spo2 and EKG visuals are live-so we're all aware.
We're getting close-arriving with a solid vitals profile, protocol advances.
All signs point to steady now. No critical changes-just let's make it to the entry.
He's holding on to me so well. Such a relief.
Infection and mobility risks are good-skin check is all negative so far.
Let's all stick to one side when we arrive. Make life easier on the ED team.
We'll flank left and load onto front ramp with the full burn sheet in tow.
Time now is estimated for full arrival-no incoming interruptions.
All set, inside and ready to wheel out your toughest EMT unit.
